title: Lesson 428
transcript: the cyber kill chain or the cyber attack chain. This is actually a concept that came from the military and military exercises. The basic idea is this. Imagine there's a tank here and there's an enemy tank on the hill over there. This tank here that wants to kill that tank over there There's a whole series of steps that have to occur in order for this tank to kill the one over there. First of all, this tank has to be able to spot, see the tank over there. Then it has to be able to rotate its turret. And it has to be able to set its gun at the right elevation. I know nothing about tanks. Then it has to be able to load some ammunition, hit the fire button. the point is there's a whole series of steps that need to occur in order for this tank to be able to kill the tank over there and that's where the term kill chain idea comes from the idea is is that in order to achieve an objective there's a whole chain of events that have to happen and if i'm the tank over here on the hill and i don't want to get shot and killed then what I want to do is think about how can I break the chain? How can I make it so I'm not spotted by the enemy tank? How can I make it so that the tank can't rotate its turret or load ammunition or whatever? So it's a concept that came from the military. Lockheed Martin took this idea of this chain of events that have to occur that must to achieve some objective. Lockheed Martin took this objective and they applied it to cybersecurity. And of course they called it the cyber kill chain or the cyber attack chain. And it's exact same underlying idea, which is, If a hacker wants to break in and do nefarious things inside of your organization, there's a whole series, there's a whole chain of events that have to occur. And so what we wanna be doing as defenders is we wanna think about what are all the links in this chain? And how can we try and break one of those links? Because we can break a link in the chain, the attack can't proceed, and we're not going to get hacked. We're not going to get compromised, which is obviously desirable. So here's what you need to know. You need to know the seven links in this chain. You need to know the order of those links. And you need to know roughly what's happening at each link. So let me walk you through all of that in detail now. And we'll start high level with just the seven links in the chain. And then in a second here, I'll show you, or in a minute, I'll show you a detailed slide with detailed information on each of the seven steps. All right, so here's the first one. Step one in the Cyberkill chain is reconnaissance. Step two is weaponization. Three, there we go, is delivery. Four is exploit. Five is installation. Six is command and control. And seven is actions. So again, I'll show you a detailed slide and I'll walk you through what each of these steps mean in just a moment here. But just staying at the high level. Remember, you need to know each of the seven links in the chain. You need to know the names of the links. And you need to understand, again, what's the whole purpose of this? This is a model that we can use as defenders to think about how can we try and protect our environment. Because if we can try and break a link in the chain, for instance, if we can prevent some malicious software from exploiting our systems, not being able to install and run on our systems, then we can break this link in the chain. And if we can break this link in the chain, the attack's not going to be able to proceed any further. The attacker's not going to be able to get into our environment and perform nefarious actions. So that's the whole purpose of this model. Think about how we as the defenders can break each link in the chain. And for the exploit, for instance, here, Typically, or one of the major ways that we can try and prevent malware from being able to exploit our systems is by patching our systems. So if we can patch our systems as quickly as possible in a timely manner, then we can try and break this link in the chain. And of course, the other thing we want to be thinking about this is we want to try and break the link as early as possible. It's probably not possible for you to entirely prevent the attackers from knowing you even exist. But, you know, if you could, that'd be great. And of course, you know, because the earlier you can break the link in the chain, the less far the attackers are going to get into their attack. And that's obviously desirable. So that's the whole idea here. And here's the details. Here's the seven links in the Cyberkill chain. Step one, as I said, or link one is reconnaissance. And this is where the attacker is researching the target organization. And specifically, one of the main things they're trying to do is figure out what systems are running in this environment and what are potential vulnerabilities in those systems that could be exploited. So this is happening during the reconnaissance. This is what's happening during reconnaissance. The attacker is reconnoitering, figuring out what systems are running, what vulnerabilities exist in the target environment. Step two, weaponization. This is where the attacker is creating some custom malware that will be able to exploit the vulnerabilities that have been identified. Step three, delivery. This is where the attacker is trying to think about how can they get the malware into the target environment. Can they get it in through a spear phishing email or a whaling email or smishing? Or maybe they could do a baiting attack by sprinkling USB sticks through the company employee parking lot and having an employee pick one up and walk that USB stick through all the layers of physical security and plug into their system. That's the point of delivery. The attacker is trying to figure out how can they get the malware into the target environment. Step four, exploit. This is where the malware is actually going to be triggered and will run in the environment, exploit some vulnerability in the system. Step five, installation. As soon as the attacker has managed to get a foothold in the target environment, they're going to want to make sure they can maintain that persistence. And that's typically by installing some sort of backdoor, which is going to make it so that even if the company patches their systems, the attackers still have a way in that can maintain that persistence. which is then going to allow them to maintain a persistent connection and start to send command and controls into the target environment, which will then, of course, allow them to perform nefarious actions, ransomware attacks, exfiltrate sensitive data, perform denial of service attacks, whatever they want to do. So... One final reminder, know all seven links in the chain, know the order of them and know roughly what's happening each link. And the whole point of this is we as defenders should be thinking through how can we break each link in this chain and how can we break the chain as early as possible to better defend our systems.
